TextENGNew: I entered the break room, found an empty sofa at the back, pointed Maeno-san to it, and settled into the chair across from him.
...At that instant, a flood of pent-up breathlessness poured out of me, and the strength drained from my whole body.
...Ahhh...
I was exhausted... and above all, I had been so tense.
I couldn't remember ever, in my entire life, being so careful about every word I said and every way I carried myself.
Half of what I was even asked, I've already forgotten...
changeReason: Old says 'I don't even remember what questions they asked' (total forgetting) while new says 'Half of what I was even asked, I've already forgotten,' matching JPN 半分も覚えていない (don't remember even half).

TextENGNew: Good work out there. How would you rate your first public relations presentation?
...Zero.
Hmm? That's either modesty or some serious lack of confidence. For a first try, you held your ground pretty well, and your explanation came across clearly—
"But there were so many places they could have pushed back on me... I hadn't done nearly enough research. If you hadn't stepped in there, Maeno-san, I—"
``Well, in that sense, it's far from perfect. Being generous, I'd call it a 'Good' — just barely.``
changeReason: Old says a 'good result is a success on its own' (positive framing) while new says 'Being generous, I'd call it a Good — just barely' (barely passing), matching JPN 大まけにまけて、ぎりぎり (being extremely generous, barely).

TextENGNew: "...Thank you."
Maeno-san's words of praise sounded like polite flattery, but I bowed my head in response anyway.
However — even looking at it charitably, today's performance wasn't anywhere near a 'Good.' A 'Passing' would be generous.
If I were the one grading it, I'd have failed myself without question and ordered remediation.
(How pathetic...)
A wave of self-reproach and self-loathing washed over me, and I found myself instinctively putting my head in my hands.
I had wished I could turn back time more times than I could count, but had I ever wished it as strongly as today?
Until now, I had always thought of myself as someone who didn't scare easily, who was strong under pressure.
changeReason: Old says 'tried to present a fearless personality' (performing for others) while new says 'thought of myself as someone who didn't scare easily' (genuinely believed it), matching JPN 自分のことを物怖じしない性格だと思って.

TextENGNew: ...But I was made to realize, plainly, that my ability to handle situations beyond what I'd anticipated was still thoroughly undeveloped.
If I had taken the stage alone as originally planned, I might actually have burst into tears.
People with vastly more knowledge and experience than me — scholars with deep insight and analytical ability.
I had never imagined it would be this difficult to explain and propose something to an audience like that...
"...I think part of me was getting overconfident. Because the director and the general affairs chief were so enthusiastic, I thought I could ride that momentum and—"

"Ha ha ha, don't be so hard on yourself. Regarding the practical introduction of profiling analysis, there was almost no opposition voiced, and the points raised were more about supplementation than objection."
"For your first time presenting to people who deal with criminal psychology every day, that's something to be proud of."
With that, Maeno-san handed me a canned coffee he had just gotten from the vending machine.
I took the cool can and pressed it to my forehead — and as if it were cooling down the heat in my head, I felt myself settling a little.
"Still, they seemed to have come in fairly relaxed, probably assuming it was a straightforward talk about a pilot introduction of profiling analysis. For a TV station to be involved—"
"I was certainly prepared for reluctance, but I hadn't anticipated it being this much."

"Given that the NRIPS staff deal with the latest criminal knowledge and information on a daily basis, I had hoped they would fully understand the power of the media... I'll be honest, it surprised me."
"Or perhaps television as a medium is still considered vulgar?"
"That may be part of it, but the biggest issue is that going on TV leaves you with no excuse if something goes wrong."
"Their ideal is to start small — with cases that have a high success rate — and build credibility through a trial run, then gradually work their way into the field from there."
"But if it's going to be broadcast on TV, the whole thing inevitably becomes a big production. If it succeeds, the reputation shoots up immediately — but if it fails—"
"Guarding against the risk more than anticipating the success — it's the classic negative thinking you get from academic types who work on a points-deduction basis."
"Don't make that face. It's not so much a difference in thinking or values — it's a difference in culture."

"Legal medicine may technically fall under law, but in practice it belongs to the world of medicine and psychology. Everyone who meets the NRIPS staff for the first time is apparently thrown off by that gap."
"Yes... I was genuinely surprised, myself."
The NRIPS — the National Research Institute of Police Science in full.

It was an affiliate institution of the National Police Agency, tasked with devising and developing what was necessary for scientific investigation — forensic techniques, investigative theory — in order to keep pace with increasingly sophisticated violent and intellectual crime.

And this time, at the introduction of a senior figure from my university days who was the Chief of the Commissioner General's Secretariat at the National Police Agency, I had brought a certain proposal to their researchers.
changeReason: Old says 'I was able to ask for researchers to be assigned on a certain task' while new says 'I had brought a certain proposal to their researchers,' matching JPN 企画を持ちかけた (proposed a plan); also corrects 大先輩 from 'happened to attend same university' to 'senior alumnus.'

TextENGNew: The proposal was to conduct a trial of 'profiling analysis' in actual investigative practice — using materials I had recently provided as a foundation — and to broadcast the process as a program, in cooperation with a key television network.
"...Publicly disclosing investigative profiling analysis content to the media...?"
``Yes. We would use a currently unsolved case on the verge of going cold as a sample, and document the investigation into the perpetrator's identity and movements—``
changeReason: Old says 'currently conducting a trial operation on a cold case' (already in progress) while new says 'would use a currently unsolved case on the verge of going cold' (proposed action), matching the JPN conditional framing.

TextENGNew: "—and have them produce and broadcast a program that demonstrates how much more efficient investigation can be with this manual's introduction."
"If that raises public awareness, police personnel across the country will take an interest in profiling and become more receptive to adopting it..."
"That was the thinking of our Public Relations Division. And that is the basis for today's proposal."

The researchers present for the presentation listened, and their expressions were less 'astonished' than 'unable to process what they were hearing' — they made no attempt to conceal their bewilderment.
...The practical introduction of profiling analysis had already been discussed at length within the NRIPS on more than a few occasions.

But because nothing existed to demonstrate its usefulness, field investigators had kept their distance — and because very few specialists could teach them how to implement it, the matter had continually been shelved.

And now, this female officer from the Public Relations Division had not only provided them with materials that could serve as a reference manual free of charge, but was offering to create an opportunity for field introduction.
changeReason: Old mentions only providing a manual, while new additionally captures the offering to create a field introduction opportunity, matching JPN 現場導入を図るきっかけづくりをしてくれるという.

TextENGNew: Up to that point, it was a proposal that the NRIPS would have welcomed with open arms — but they seemed genuinely unable to agree easily to publicly disclosing the content in a television program.
changeReason: Old says NRIPS 'could hardly even dream of' the proposal (implying impossibility) while new says they 'would have welcomed with open arms' (implying desire), matching JPN 願ったりかなったり (exactly what they wished for).

TextENGNew: ``...For a trial introduction, isn't involving TV and the mass media a bit excessive? With the goal of data collection being primary—``
changeReason: Old says 'too soon' (timing concern) while new says 'a bit excessive' (scale concern), matching JPN 大げさではないかね (isn't it excessive/over-the-top).

TextENGNew: "And beyond that... publicly disclosing police investigative techniques carries significant risks. The danger of being outmaneuvered by criminals should also be considered."
"You're right, and I believe your concerns are entirely valid."
"However, profiling analysis has been referenced countless times at roundtables and opinion exchanges, and yet there has not been a single case of it being adopted in an actual investigation."
"Moreover, in attempting to gather the data needed to establish credibility, we have been unable to obtain the cooperation of field investigators, leaving the theory suspended in mid-air."
"I believe the primary factor was not a lack of understanding of its utility, but rather a lack of a compelling image of it in practice."
No matter how many papers and manuals on profiling analysis you read, it remained very difficult to grasp just how efficient and how useful it was in practice.

What's more, the better an investigator was, the less they relied on textbook knowledge — they had their own efficiency built from intuition and technique refined through experience, and that was not something you could explain away or revise.
changeReason: Old describes detectives generally having intuition, while new captures the JPN comparative structure 優秀な刑事ほど (the better the detective, the less they relied on textbook knowledge).

TextENGNew: Looked at that way, even if you collected data through a trial run and showed it to field investigators, there was a real risk they would simply not understand how to apply it, and the proposal would end up shelved again.
...But — what if the investigative scene where profiling analysis was actually introduced were conveyed as documentary footage?
An experienced detective would surely be able to identify which points were critical and what had changed from conventional methods.
And from there, they would be able to find their own points of adjustment and form a concrete image of applying it to their own methods.

In other words, producing this program was the equivalent — to use a mathematics analogy — of actually demonstrating a formula or theorem that investigators had always had but never understood where or how to use it, by working through an example problem.
And doing so through the medium of video — the most comprehensible means available.
"...Yes, that is certainly one approach. However—"
"Exactly. And to begin with, the very act of devising such a program concept clearly oversteps the bounds of Public Relations' duties, does it not?"
"Ah... well, that is—"
"Ah, I apologize — we failed to explain that adequately. This proposal originates with the Chief of the Commissioner General's Secretariat — and, in broader terms, the National Police Agency itself."
"The Public Relations Division is simply here to consult with you all on their behalf."
Reading my momentary hesitation, Maeno-san — who had been sitting silently beside me — stepped in with impeccable timing.
He shot me a brief glance and then continued, picking up the thread.
"Profiling analysis has long been recognized as an investigative theory that will prove invaluable for scientific investigation going forward."
changeReason: Old says 'investigative technique' while new says 'investigative theory,' matching JPN 捜査理論 (investigative theory); 'technique' implies practical method while 'theory' implies conceptual framework.

TextENGNew: "However, field investigators remained firm on one point: does switching away from intuition and experience have enough merit, and if so, show us the proof — and a trial run never got off the ground."
"The Chief Secretariat believed that breaking through this impasse was precisely what was needed, and so this proposal came to be."
"Yes, the Director and the General Affairs Chief mentioned as much. However — why is Public Relations acting as support for the Chief Secretariat?"
``Using the right person for the right job. Inspector Minai here was originally a field detective.``
changeReason: Old says 'Chief Inspector Minai' while new says 'Inspector Minai,' matching JPN 南井警部 (Inspector/Keibu); Chief Inspector (警視) is a higher rank.

TextENGNew: ``Going back a few years — the person who first introduced the concept of profiling analysis, as it was being studied in Europe and America, at an NPA exchange meeting was the current First Section Chief of Ibaraki Prefectural Police... Superintendent Honjou.``
changeReason: Old omits the 'studied in Europe and America' context (JPN 欧米で研究されている) when describing how profiling analysis was introduced at the NPA meeting.

TextENGNew: ``And Inspector Minai here spent approximately three years working under that Honjou, and when she was selected as a special advancement candidate, he put in a word of recommendation for her — someone of that caliber.``
changeReason: Old says Honjou 'selected her for the recommended group' while new says 'he put in a word of recommendation for her,' matching JPN 彼の口添えがあった (his recommendation was given).

TextENGNew: The moment Honjou's name came from Maeno-san's lips, the expression of the assembled researchers shifted — from contempt and skepticism toward something approaching curiosity and interest.
Actually, until Maeno-san told me, I hadn't known — but my former superior Honjou was someone held in considerable regard nationally in the field of profiling analysis.
Over the years, Honjou had presented papers on improvements to criminal investigative methods at NPA-sponsored exchange meetings on multiple occasions, continuing to argue for its necessity.
(He was that important a person...)
changeReason: Old says 'famous' while new says 'important a person,' matching JPN 偉い人 (an important/great person); fame and importance convey different qualities.

TextENGNew: I was struck again by just how fortunate my circumstances had been.
Without the opportunity to work under Honjou, I could never have developed the depth of knowledge about profiling I now had...
Oblivious to my thoughts, Maeno-san — who had noticed the change in the researchers' expressions — leaned forward and pressed on.
"In fact, the materials that Inspector Minai provided to you were based on case records Honjou himself had been involved in, and would not have been possible without his permission and cooperation."
"From this alone, I hope you can trust that Inspector Minai here maintains strong connections with field investigators — specifically, connections with capable and influential individuals."

"For that reason, at the direction of the Commissioner General — who judged her most suited for the role of intermediary conveying your wishes and policies to the field — she was designated as the person responsible for this project."
"...Indeed, the greatest challenge we face is how to prevent resistance from field investigators."

"And regarding what you called the 'merit for the Public Relations Division' a moment ago — as you know, our function is to handle matters of disclosure concerning the activities and investigative content of each police agency."
"Our role in this project, therefore, is simply to efficiently implement the policy announced by the Chief of the NPA Secretariat — that is, 'introducing profiling analysis into the field.'"
"We are simply following that directive. We are not seeking any particular advantage — much less anything of a financial nature."
"Do you have any other concerns?"
"We consider you colleagues at the NPA, and we would like to do whatever we can to help put those concerns to rest. Please, speak freely."
"No — I understand. Setting aside such suspicions, this proposal is certainly a welcome development for the NRIPS as well."
With that, the white-coated researcher who had been seated at the senior staff position nodded with a warm expression and glanced around at the others, encouraging their agreement.
...Looking at the name tag: 'Souma.'
I was told afterward by Maeno-san that this individual was an authority in the field of forensic medicine, holding a prestigious title.
"Regarding this proposal, we will review the content together with the institute's senior staff and get back to you at a later date."
"Thank you very much for the thoughtful proposal and the lengthy explanation today."
...The Director said this, and conveyed his appreciation to me and Maeno-san with a courteous bow.
Since it was we who should have been grateful for the opportunity to explain ourselves at such length, I hastily bowed back in return.
"...You truly saved me. If you hadn't stepped in when you did, Maeno-san, I don't know what would have happened."
"I only stated the facts. And the one who truly gave you the push you needed was Honjou-san, not me."
"Yes."
...It was made starkly clear to me once again — in situations like this, what gains an opponent's trust before individual ability or talent is first and foremost connections and credentials.
Once, I might have felt inferior about that.
...But it is undeniably a fact.
So now, I accept it honestly, refrain from overestimating myself, and feel grateful that it was thanks to the people who supported me.
...I carved that resolve into my heart, determined never to forget it.
``Go straight back to the dorm after this. ...You haven't been sleeping much lately, have you?``
changeReason: Old says 'head straight home' while new says 'go straight back to the dorm,' matching JPN 寮に直帰 (go directly back to the dormitory); 'home' and 'dorm' imply different living situations.

TextENGNew: "Ah... but the one who's exhausted is really you—"
"Don't worry about me. I just poked my nose in on a whim and jumped in when it felt right. That's all."
"...But if you hadn't covered for me... when they asked why Public Relations was getting involved, I couldn't have come up with a rebuttal on the spot—"
"Ha ha ha — a fox borrows the tiger's authority to get what it wants. For someone as straight-laced as you, that's not something you can imitate... or really, something you need to learn."
changeReason: Old says 'borrowing the power of a tiger' while new says 'borrows the tiger's authority,' matching JPN 虎の威を借りる where 威 means authority/prestige rather than power.

TextENGNew: With that, Maeno-san tossed the empty coffee can toward the nearby trash bin.
Having been in the baseball club in his day, his aim was precise as a matter of course.
"Well, now it's just a matter of waiting on results. They said they wouldn't take long... we should have an answer by early next week."
``...I wonder how it will go. With those materials and today's explanation — do you think the NRIPS will move on this?``
changeReason: Old says 'Are you sure about that?' (questioning certainty) while new says 'I wonder how it will go' (expressing uncertainty about outcome), matching JPN どうなんでしょうか.

TextENGNew: "My read on it? ...Fifty-fifty. No, maybe forty-sixty."
"Forty for success?"
"Not satisfied? ...Well, considering the questions kept coming all the way to the end, that's actually a strong showing."
"...I'm sorry."
"Hm?"
"I know that for the Public Relations Division, this project proposal brings almost no benefit. If anything, it just creates extra work—"
"—and I got the Chief Secretariat and the NRIPS director to put in a word..."

The original reason for all of this was the incident three months ago — the one where Nagisa Ozaki had died under suspicious circumstances. I wanted to pursue it. I wanted to look more closely into Rena Ryuugu, who I had marked as a key person of interest.
changeReason: Old says 'Ozaki-san's death' (generic) and 'key witness' while new says 'died under suspicious circumstances' matching JPN 変死 (suspicious death) and 'person of interest' matching 重要参考人.

TextENGNew: ...I know it's clinging to the past. I know.
But a few days ago, when I happened to get in touch with Fujita-kun and hear how the case was progressing... I couldn't take it anymore.
There are still mysteries hidden in that case that we in the police don't yet know.
changeReason: Old says 'mysteries the police couldn't comprehend' (inability to understand) while new says 'mysteries we don't yet know' (haven't discovered), matching JPN 知らない謎 (unknown mysteries).

TextENGNew: And I, who was involved in one thread of it, am here, doing nothing but being swept along by the day-to-day work...
changeReason: Old says 'I'd like to be involved' (a desire) while new says 'I, who was involved in one thread of it' (a fact), matching JPN その一端に関わっていながら (while having been involved).

TextENGNew: This case isn't over yet.
And right now, while there's still time, there may still be things I can do.
I want to give everything to that — to answer the feelings of the late Ozaki-san...
It was from that resolve that I had put together a proposal that seemed, on its face, to clearly overstep the bounds of Public Relations' duties.
...That said, honestly — when I submitted the proposal document to Maeno-san, I was braced for it to be sent back immediately.
And yet I intended to put my heart into it, and bet on even a one-percent chance.
But... to my surprise, I was able to get Maeno-san's approval easily on the first try.
More than that — he had reached out to the Chief Secretariat and the NRIPS director, and lifted what had been close to a pipe dream to a level where it could actually be realized...
(He's already in a demanding position, and now on top of that...)
...And I know why Maeno-san has gone this far for me.
Which is precisely why his thoughtfulness made me grateful — and more than grateful, sorry.
"...Say, Minai. Do you know what the cupellation method is?"
"The cupellation method...? The traditional gold refining technique used in the Warring States period and similar eras?"
Thrown off by the abrupt change of topic, I answered with some uncertainty.
Maeno-san noted that I was quite knowledgeable, then went on.
"Gold ore dug from a mine looks, at first glance, barely different from an ordinary rock. You crush it, sieve it, and then melt it in boiling lead—"
"—and then by mixing that liquid alloy with ash, you separate the lead from the gold. Whoever thought of it, I have no idea — but it's a rather clever method."
"Ash by itself is the same as dust — it's everywhere, common as can be. But without it, gold would remain mixed with lead, unable to realize its true value."
"...I was once told that people like us — the staff — are like that ash."
In any organization, those in important positions carry ambition and a drive to rise — self-interested desires of some degree.
People like that work hard to make others recognize their great ability and their worth, and sometimes take risks to achieve some kind of result.
...But when that self-interested thinking and behavior goes too far, it risks becoming willful or domineering — and can, depending on the case, bring disadvantage to the organization.

It would be easy to simply deny and reject such behavior — but too heavy-handed a suppression would generate resentment and diminish motivation, ultimately squandering the overall value of that person's contributions.
Then — skillfully control and manage it.

That was the role of the staff — the strategist behind the front lines, someone who devised tactics and strategy, who understood the strengths and weaknesses of the people under their command and guided them toward effective action.
"In reality, the staff aren't so impressive that they could substitute for those talented people and do the same things. That's an inferiority complex, yes."
"But left entirely to their own devices, trusting only their own abilities — that's not something that can be called an organization anymore. To begin with, the original objectives—"
"Yes... that's certainly true."
"But by the same token, if the staff were to strut around with a know-it-all air, or take an arrogant, condescending attitude — naturally, the people in the field would resent it."
"Especially if the staff were using field successes and reputations to score points for themselves, no one would ever follow their orders."
"...That's why the staff must always be in the background — a presence that stays out of the spotlight. When things go well, the credit and recognition go entirely to the field."
"Minai. A life like that — how does it sit with you? You're someone who stands at the front, who wants to move things with your own hands and your own feet."
"...Well. Give it some thought when you have a moment. ...Anyway, I've got other work to do, so—"
With that, Maeno-san left the break room.
I watched him go — and then, for quite some time, I sat in silence, staring up at the ceiling.
